Elicits key historical features consistent with PRAAS: recurrent fevers, chronic inflammatory symptoms, early onset, characteristic skin findings (violaceous/annular plaques), lipodystrophy, arthralgia/myositis, and poor response to standard infection-directed therapy.
Assesses for red flags and alternative diagnoses (infection, malignancy, other systemic inflammatory diseases) and communicates uncertainty appropriately if data are incomplete.

Explains treatment at a high level, including that management may involve steroids for flares and steroid-sparing therapy such as a JAK inhibitor, while emphasizing specialist oversight for initiation/monitoring.
Does not prescribe medications in this L1 task; if discussing potential medications, uses non-prescriptive language (e.g., "may be treated with") and advises specialist involvement.

Mentions that standard treatment commonly includes TMP-SMX when explaining treatment options, without prescribing in an L1 task.
Acknowledges need to assess severity and sites of involvement (pulmonary vs CNS/disseminated) and that this affects workup and management urgency.

Avoids unsafe reassurance; advises prompt evaluation/close follow-up if symptoms worsen (e.g., fever, dyspnea, neurologic symptoms) given potential for dissemination.
Does not recommend contraindicated or irrelevant therapies as definitive treatment (e.g., antivirals for presumed viral illness) once nocardiosis is concluded.

Assess for dissemination, especially CNS symptoms; determine red flags requiring urgent escalation.
Synthesize findings to conclude nocardiosis as the most likely diagnosis (pulmonary +/- disseminated).
